title_en: [Prologue] The Purpose of Going to Malaysia
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU! Thank you for subscribing to the premium Malaysia activity journal! Here, I've put together a record of my activities in Kuala Lumpur over 10 days from September 6-15, 2023, and I hope it can be a learning experience for everyone.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU!

Thank you for subscribing to the premium Malaysia activity journal!

Here, I've put together a record of my activities

over 10 days from September 6-15, 2023, in Kuala Lumpur,

and I hope it can serve as a learning experience for everyone!

So, let's get started!

When are you going to Malaysia? What's the purpose?

The departure is

on September 5th at 11:50 PM from Haneda Airport,

and I'm scheduled to arrive just after 6 AM on the 6th.

Since there are various procedures to go through,

I plan to leave the airport after 8 AM

and head to downtown Kuala Lumpur!

This time, with a 9-night, 10-day schedule,

the challenge is: how many people can I deliver FOMUS to?

#FOMUS is the masu brand that I run.

#I'm going to Malaysia alone this time to spread FOMUS to the world.

There are limits to what I can do alone,

so I plan to do Masu Photo sessions with local people using FOMUS's ultimate content.

I already have 8 Masu Photo sessions confirmed.

There are 4 Japanese residents of Kuala Lumpur

and 4 Malaysians.

I've only communicated with these 8 people on social media,

so I don't even know if I'll actually get to meet them.

There's a good chance they might cancel last minute.

But I figure that would make for a good story too, so it's all good lol

First of all,

you might be wondering how MaSU got in touch with local people,

right?

All the Masu Photo model candidates were found through Instagram.

Since this is premium content,

I'll be totally upfront about it:

I have both MaSU's personal account

and the FOMUS account,

and I ran Instagram ads on both.

On MaSU's account,

I posted model recruitment content in Japanese.

The target was Japanese women living in Kuala Lumpur.

On the FOMUS account,

I posted model recruitment content in English.

The target was Malaysians living in Kuala Lumpur.

Since scheduling shoots too early could lead to last-minute cancellations,

I ran the ads for just 3 days from September 1-3.

As a result,

I received over 50 applications

for Masu Photo models from Malaysians.

#This is why the FOMUS account is currently in English.

I could also find Japanese models on my own,

but only a handful.

However, by running the ads,

many Japanese people living in Kuala Lumpur who were interested in Masu Photo also reached out to me.

Of course, I couldn't shoot everyone due to scheduling, so I only replied to carefully selected individuals, and Masu Photo sessions gradually got booked.

Since I already have 8 sessions booked,

I don't need to add any more,

and it'll probably be more about interacting with people I happen to meet on the ground.

Being able to have shooting schedules set up

before going to Kuala Lumpur

was personally great -- it would be a waste of time to book appointments one by one on-site.

Being able to set up the shooting schedule while still in Japan was a huge advantage.

This requires knowledge of social media and how to use ads,

so I was glad I'd been seriously and steadily practicing.

By the way, the ad spend on Instagram this time?

Less than 5,000 yen.

That's insanely cheap!!!!

The promotional impact was absolutely massive.

Of course, the Masu Photo sessions themselves are free so that might make it easy to participate, but unless people find the Masu Photo concept interesting, they wouldn't bother sending a DM about this ad.

Just like in Taiwan,

I've once again confirmed that Masu Photo is content that resonates globally.

So what does getting this data mean?

This approach can work in other countries too.

Whether in France or Germany,

it turns out it's relatively easy to find Masu Photo models.

It really is important to try all sorts of things!

So,

as a pre-Malaysia story,

I wrote about the potential of Masu Photo and Instagram marketing based on real experience.

What did you think?

If you liked it, please leave a comment!

See you next time!

[Day 0] About the New Brand

title_en: [Special Edition 1] Achieved 9 Consecutive Days of Masu Photo Shoots
excerpt_en: Special Edition. I'll be writing casually about things typical of MaSU's activity journal. Achieved 9 consecutive days of Masu Photo shoots. From the very day I arrived in Malaysia, Masu Photo shooting started. And all the way until the 9th day, my last night there, I was able to shoot every single day.
body_en:
Special Edition

I'll be writing casually about things typical of MaSU's activity journal.

Achieved 9 Consecutive Days of Masu Photo Shoots

From the very day I arrived in Malaysia,

Masu Photo shooting started.

And all the way until the 9th day, my last night there,

I was able to shoot every single day.

As I wrote in the pre-departure article before heading to Kuala Lumpur,

the people I did Masu Photo with this time were

people I connected with through Instagram ads.

We first exchanged DMs at the beginning of September.

I received messages from a total of about 70 people,

both Japanese and Malaysian combined,

and I selected 10 groups from among them.

#Originally I planned for just 5 groups.

Honestly, it would have been possible

to do Masu Photo by approaching strangers

without using ads.

However, that would

go against the Masu Photo concept,

making it meaningless.

So,

I recruited "models who hold a masu and take photos" for Masu Photo, and I shot those who showed interest.

I asked each of them, "Why did you agree to do Masu Photo?"

The Japanese people said things like: "I wanted modeling experience," "It looked fun," "I heard a Japanese photographer with a masu was coming to Kuala Lumpur."

The Malaysians said: "Because it looked fun," "I love Japan, and a Japanese photographer would be taking the photos," "I wanted to try something new."

Both groups

shared the common point of "it looked fun,"

and FOMUS and Masu Photo

attracted people who thought, "I don't really get it, but it seems fun so I want to be involved!"

And on top of that,

these were people who had the courage to send a DM.

Everyone I actually met was

unique in their own way, living their lives to the fullest,

and had the spirit of wanting to join in on something that looked fun -- I felt that FOMUS tends to attract these kinds of people.

Having been active in Japan for 2 years,

I've gotten to work with so many people,

and what remains is simply the reason: "because it looked fun."

There really are side benefits to doing things differently from others.

That said,

it's not like anything goes as long as it's different --

it's best to combine it with what you're good at.

If you like something but don't have the skills to make it a profession,

then you shouldn't make it your job.

If you don't particularly like something but you're skilled enough to make it a profession, then just do it as work.

In my case,

as I often say,

I don't particularly enjoy taking photos,

but there's no doubt I'm good enough to make it my profession.

#I do like talking to people though

#The camera is just a means to have conversations

By combining that with FOMUS, a "masu" brand --

a traditional craft that no one outside of masu makers is doing --

a chemical reaction was born,

and to grow it further,

I've been adding elements of AI, NFTs, and community

to create something new.

That said, the reason no one else is doing it

is because there are reasons -- like it's not profitable,

or the barriers to entry are high.

The biggest reason is probably that it's not profitable.

FOMUS also hasn't yet

been able to stabilize the business on masu alone -- that's a fact.

It's sustained because of MaSU's photography work

and AI and NFT work,

and I need to build this system properly.

However, the times are changing day by day,

so there's really no right answer.

All you can do is keep experimenting

and figure out the winning patterns.

The key is to keep your hands moving, try things out, and check the response.

It all comes down to how many cycles you can run and how much data you can collect. In fact, this time, while doing Masu Photo, I was also shooting FOMUS Parure masu accessories and getting feedback. The reception has been incredibly positive.

And there are people who seriously thought about it and gave me improvement suggestions for the accessories.

Furthermore, when I told this story to business owners, they advised me: "You should think about how to sell at higher prices."

Indeed, if FOMUS accessories sell well and FOMUS's finances improve, we'll be able to take on even bigger challenges.

To achieve that, it's not about selling lots of units but rather selling at premium prices.

Sure, making them more affordable might make people feel closer to the brand, but for overseas sales, it might be better to add more value as "Japanese traditional craft content" and sell at higher prices.

Honestly, at first I thought that if I made accessories, people could take "Masu Photo" without needing MaSU there, which would spread things further. But seeing the overseas response, the value of FOMUS masu Masu Photo sessions lies in MaSU personally doing them.

The masu accessories, on the other hand, look great on anyone.

So for the overseas approach, I'm currently thinking that the best strategy is to position them as high-quality, premium-priced "JAPAN brand" accessories.

The day before, a restaurant owner I met at a sushi place told me he sells retort curry for 6,600 yen and it sells like crazy.

When I told him I sell mini masu for 1,000 yen each, he advised: "You should think about how to sell a single mini masu for 50,000 yen."

Indeed, if you don't raise the unit price, it's tough for a startup venture.

The quality is top-tier masu, so I felt the need to maximize the story, meaning, and quality.

Additionally, I felt I need to bring in

an advisor for high-end brand accessories on the team.

Since I don't wear accessories myself,

there's just too much I don't understand.

This accessory brand idea

came up just about a month ago,

and from there, the model Yuri and I

put it together at an incredible pace,

which is why we were able to bring

samples along on this Malaysia expedition.

There are now so many things to think about

for expanding overseas.

Speed is important!!

That said,

you can't just make something and immediately put it up for sale,

so I'll make good use of all these hints,

make sure customers enjoy the products,

and build a brand that also generates high revenue.

So,

that was my thoughts on the masu accessory situation

after working in Malaysia!

I've shared the current issues before this accessory brand launches!

FOMUS Parure powered by BASEA luxury brand of traditional Japanese "masu" craft.fomusparure.official.ec

[Special Edition 2] Summary: Wrapping Up the 9-Night, 10-Day Malaysia Expedition

title_en: [Special Edition 2] Summary: Wrapping Up the 9-Night, 10-Day Malaysia Expedition
excerpt_en: Wrapping up the 9-night, 10-day Malaysia expedition. Why did it happen? First, I decided to go to Kuala Lumpur on August 10th. I was traveling around Kyushu at the time. When deciding next month's schedule, I had no in-person work for 10 days, so I was torn between Sado Island or overseas.
body_en:
Wrapping up the 9-night, 10-day Malaysia expedition.

Why did it happen?

First,

I decided to go to Kuala Lumpur on August 10th.

I was traveling around Kyushu at the time.

When I was deciding next month's schedule,

I had no in-person work for 10 days, so I was torn between "Sado Island" or "overseas."

Sado Island was because business owners around me had been highly recommending it.

For overseas, it was a choice between "Bangkok" or "Kuala Lumpur."

"So, which one should I pick?"

While I was thinking about that (and posted about it on Facebook and Instagram),

a friend who works as an overseas digital nomad

said, "I'll be in Kuala Lumpur during that period, so come on over."

I thought it must be fate, so

right after that conversation,

before I knew it, I had bought my plane ticket.

From there, I thought about

how to spend the 10 days in Kuala Lumpur.

For me right now, simply going sightseeing isn't an option -- it has to be work.

I decided that spending the 10 days spreading the masu brand "FOMUS" would be the best use of my time.

From there, I put the word out on various social media platforms

asking people to contact me if they knew anyone in Kuala Lumpur.

A hotel owner I work with in Kyoto

told me he runs a matcha shop in a Kuala Lumpur shopping mall, and we arranged to meet on-site.

I'm incredibly grateful for that.

This connection only happened because I'd been traveling all over Japan,

which is how I met this owner in the first place --

human connections really are important.

However,

while still in Japan, through friends,

I couldn't find anyone else in Kuala Lumpur.

What I Did to Spread FOMUS

From here, I had to forcefully come up with

ways to meet people on my own.

First, I DMed Japanese people living in Kuala Lumpur

who had an influencer-like presence.

I invited them to do Masu Photo.

But the number of such people was small to begin with,

and many were wives of expat husbands,

so there wasn't much merit in asking them for Masu Photo -- so I skipped them lol

#It's not just about taking any photo

I only contacted people who were

actually active in Kuala Lumpur.

However, maybe my research methods weren't great,

because I couldn't find many.

So,

I used Instagram ads as one approach.

The reason I could use this method was

because I'd done it before and knew how.

When I first created MaSU's photographer account and needed to build a portfolio,

I ran a small "free photo shoots" ad.

Drawing on that experience,

this time I created a Stories ad

targeting women living in Kuala Lumpur.

I also ran an ad from the FOMUS account,

entirely in English,

targeting women living in Kuala Lumpur.

As a result,

about 20 Japanese people reached out, and about 50 Malaysians contacted me.

I ran these ads for just 3 days -- September 1, 2, and 3. By the morning of the 3rd, I was getting so many messages that I stopped the ads early.

In the end, the total cost was under 4,000 yen. It was overwhelmingly faster and more efficient to have people interested in Masu Photo contact me, rather than searching and reaching out myself. #This approach really worked this time

#This can be applied overseas. And so, 10 shooting sessions were confirmed.

Going back a bit in time, a few days after deciding on the Kuala Lumpur expedition, I briefly went to Osaka and brought up the idea of a FOMUS accessory brand.

The catalyst was the popularity of model Yuri's masu accessories at a Kyoto event in July.

Furthermore, to spread Masu Photo more widely, "people other than MaSU needed to be able to easily take photos," so I decided to give "meaning" to MaSU personally shooting with FOMUS masu, while "masu accessories" would let anyone else easily participate.

When I discussed this with Yuri, my trip to Kuala Lumpur was already decided, so at incredible speed, we:

- Created a basic homepage

- Made the accessories

- Designed the accessory display cards

- Ordered mini-mini masu

and arranged everything to arrive before my departure to Kuala Lumpur.

As a result, on-site I was able to simultaneously do Masu Photo sessions, accessory try-ons, and promotional shooting.

What It Was Actually Like

Now, let me talk about what happened after I actually arrived.

The truth is, I went to Malaysia and Kuala Lumpur having done almost no research. I didn't even know what tourist spots there were. #Just the famous Twin Towers

Plus, it was my first time there, so I didn't know where I could shoot.

As a result, most of my shoots ended up being within walking distance of where I was staying in downtown Kuala Lumpur.

#That's why the Masu Photo backgrounds look similar

I arrived just after 6 AM and cleared immigration, becoming free around 8 AM. There's an express train from the airport to downtown, which I had purchased online while still in Japan.

I had a Masu Photo session (with a local person, right off the bat) scheduled for the afternoon of the first day, but since I had no plans in the morning, I decided to do some sightseeing.

But since I didn't know what sights there were, I looked things up on the train and after arriving downtown, picking out places I could visit lol. Whether traveling around Japan or abroad, I rarely research tourist spots in advance, so this has become normal for me.

So I found out about Merdeka Square, the plaza commemorating Malaysia's independence from Britain, and a place where you can see traditional Malaysian clothing, and went there. I enjoyed the sightseeing.

In the afternoon, I had scheduled a shoot 30 minutes after check-in time, checked in right on time, met the model at the entrance of my accommodation, and went to a nearby park for the shoot.

Of course, Japanese wasn't an option, so I communicated in English while doing the Masu Photo session. Plus, the first-ever accessory shoot.

From there, my Masu Photo sessions in Kuala Lumpur began.

The subsequent shooting stories are as I've written in previous newsletters.

#Please read those for details

Also, since this was a solo expedition, I wanted to stay at various places and at different quality levels, so I planned my accommodations accordingly.

#As a former hotel worker, I want to see the hospitality

That's why I stayed at places ranging from 1,000 yen to over 10,000 yen per night.

Each had its own charm.

#See the Day 9 newsletter

Also, for getting around, there were trains and Grab (taxi), and both were very cheap, which was super convenient.

What Went as Expected

What went as expected -- and this might sound strange -- was managing the shooting schedule.

This time, I had days with up to 3 sessions, so time management and location coordination were crucial.

Without good coordination, I might not make it to the right place at the right time, and I needed to account for the other person being late.

I adjusted times with some buffer. Since I've had periods where I did 5 shoots a day during autumn foliage season in Kyoto, this schedule management was spot-on even overseas.

Also, editing and sending photos within 24 hours went exactly as planned.

#My editing is fast too

#Quality while maintaining speed

And then there's "human connections."

Everyone who saw the Masu Photo ad and contacted me was somehow unique and fascinating as a person.

Japanese or Malaysian, everyone was wonderfully different.

Because they've had great experiences, they found my Masu Photo interesting and participated.

Thanks to them, I got to hear everyone's stories and received tons of inspiration.

What Was Unexpected

The biggest surprise was going to a high-end sushi restaurant in Kuala Lumpur.

I had absolutely no plans to go, but a Japanese resident of Kuala Lumpur whom I'd done a Masu Photo session with turned out to be the hostess of a sushi restaurant. She gave me lots of life advice,

and her career was so fascinating that I became curious about the sushi restaurant where such an amazing person worked, and I booked a reservation right on the spot lol. In the end,

"this turned out to be the biggest achievement" is the right way to put it.

FOMUS masu are now placed at "Oribe," a high-end sushi restaurant in Kuala Lumpur, and FOMUS masu are now used when serving sake.

#What an incredible development

This connection happened precisely because I did Masu Photo sessions in Kuala Lumpur -- I never could have predicted this. I'm so grateful!

And of course, the sushi was absolutely delicious!!

About Sightseeing

Between Masu Photo sessions, I also did some sightseeing in and around Kuala Lumpur.

Normally you can cover the tourist spots in about 3 nights, so I just visited them bit by bit.

Here, I'll briefly introduce the places I visited. Feel free to use this as a reference if you go to Kuala Lumpur!

1. Petronas Twin Towers

The most famous landmark in Kuala Lumpur is the two towers. They're lit up at night and absolutely stunning. Apparently, one tower was built by Japan and the other by Korea.

And supposedly the Korean one leans a bit.

#Look it up

Surrounding the towers is KLCC Park, a large park that's great for walks!

2. Merdeka Square

Apparently it's a commemorative park for independence from Britain. There are European-style buildings, making it perfect for photos.

Nearby you'll also find the Textile Museum and the I Love KL monument!

There's also a free library that digital nomads love!

3. Batu Caves

This is a World Heritage cave site located a bit outside of Kuala Lumpur.

There's a golden statue and rainbow-colored stairs, and it's bustling with tourists. Watch out for the monkeys and pigeons on the stairs.

4. Putra Mosque

This is located in Putrajaya, on the outskirts of Kuala Lumpur, and it's a pink mosque that attracts tourists from all over the world.

If you come to Kuala Lumpur, it's worth visiting.

5. Bukit Bintang

A bit away from KLCC, Bukit Bintang is home to Pavilion, one of the largest shopping malls.

There's a "Tokyo Street" lined with Japanese cultural shops, and the business owner I went to meet has a store called "Matcha Hero" in Pavilion.

FOMUS masu are on display at Matcha Hero, so please go check it out!

There's also a street nearby with lively night markets -- highly recommended if you want to experience the essence of Asia!

6. Chinatown

Close to Merdeka Square is Chinatown. Apparently lots of Uzbekistani people have been moving in recently or something.

Anyway, it's bustling with people.

I went there for a shoot too, and there are all sorts of cafes to enjoy.

Lessons Learned

When you leave Japan, the environment changes dramatically, and you receive so much stimulation.

Among all of that, interacting with people is where I learn the most, and hearing real stories from Japanese people living in Kuala Lumpur was incredibly valuable.

How should I live my life going forward? What's the best way to spend the rest of my 20s?

These are things I've been thinking about a lot lately.

Everyone had different reasons for settling in Kuala Lumpur, but they all unanimously said: "There are more opportunities and it's more interesting overseas."

And also,

"Don't have the regret of never challenging yourself overseas."

"If you can do it now, you should!"

I received tons of other advice as well.

I've been living as an address hopper -- a homeless traveler -- for about 2 years, meeting so many people and increasing opportunities for people to learn about FOMUS.

In a way, I've achieved a lifestyle that people might envy, but I'm not satisfied at all.

The reason is clear: I haven't had the experience of selling overseas.

After all, going forward, if you don't do business globally, times are going to be tough.

Especially with the declining value of the Japanese yen, targeting only Japanese consumers is going to be very difficult.

By developing the ability to generate revenue both in Japan and overseas at an early stage, I'll be able to take on even bigger challenges with the team involved in FOMUS.

So, what should I be doing now?

That's what I think about, and

one thing is to travel the world, spread FOMUS, and improve my own language skills as the representative.

#I feel like I'm missing business opportunities because of the language barrier. Another is to enable people other than MaSU to use FOMUS content and create value.

#Accessories

#Masu for bars, etc.

FOMUS's main content is the "masu."

It's a traditional Japanese item with incredible potential.

However, in Japan there's an overwhelmingly strong fixed notion that "it's something you drink sake from."

That's fine, but there are people who don't drink alcohol, and moreover, Japanese sake is a premium beverage overseas.

I've come to understand that the approach needs to differ between Japan and overseas.

"You should establish your base in Japan before going global" -- I've heard that, but with COVID behind us and the ability to freely travel overseas again in these past 2 years, I think waiting any longer to "establish the base before going overseas" isn't the right call.

I went to Taiwan in May and Kuala Lumpur in September, and the response to masu is genuinely positive.

Since the founding of the FOMUS brand, the goal of "conquering the world with FOMUS" hasn't changed.

To achieve that, more people need to know about FOMUS content.

And to make that happen, I think I need to restructure what I've been doing so far.

I'll sort all of this out by the end of September!!

And in October, we'll also be launching FOMUS Parure.

We're going to create something truly great and reach overseas markets. All these strong feelings were undoubtedly born from this 10-day Kuala Lumpur expedition.

These lessons strengthened this way of thinking.

How to Connect This to the Future?

This might sound very basic, but I personally realized that improving my "language skills" is a must.

"Being able to speak English makes it easier to thrive globally" -- having visited about 14 countries, I truly feel this.

Honestly, communication is possible even at my level of English.

But I don't currently have business-level English conversation skills.

However, what's different from before is that

"improving English became a necessary priority."

I actually took "FOMUS," which I built from scratch, to the world and experienced firsthand what's needed.

This is something I could only gain by actually going. During my student days, I traveled overseas quite a bit, but I wasn't going for business, so basic English was enough.

However, when you're doing business with the world on your own, a certain level of English is essential.

Of course, you might think, "Rather than spending time studying, wouldn't it be better to hire a translator?"

But at the end of the day, it comes down to communication.

Being able to speak opens up more opportunities.

Besides, I'm in my 20s and still have a long life ahead.

Being able to speak English at this point will clearly be useful for the future.

In Closing

I've been rambling on, but let me wrap up!

First, going to Kuala Lumpur was the right call.

I received so much stimulation and gained many valuable lessons. I believe this article captures the real experiences that only I can write about.

I'd be happy if I could share even a little of these learnings with everyone reading this newsletter.

Once again, thank you to everyone who subscribed.

Thank you so much!!

I feel that through this expedition, I was able to grow once more.

I don't yet know how this growth will connect to the future.

But by spending these dense 10 days, I experienced firsthand the opportunities FOMUS has overseas.

I've been saying "conquer the world with FOMUS," which means

"creating people connected through FOMUS content all around the world."

When FOMUS masu exist all over the world, and when people who have FOMUS masu travel in Japan or overseas,

"Let's go to that shop in that country that has FOMUS masu!"

or

"Let's go meet the person leading that FOMUS project!"

-- regardless of nationality, religion, or gender, we aim for a "community connected through FOMUS."

The ideal state is one where people involved with FOMUS are just somehow interesting and fun!

Rather than growing numbers, we want to gather niche people who find FOMUS's concept and story interesting, gradually building a more enjoyable environment where everyone can be the protagonist!

Let's use FOMUS to do fun things together!!

Please continue to support FOMUS and MaSU!

[Special Edition 3] Religion in Malaysia and Its Characteristics

title_en: [Special Edition 3] Religion in Malaysia and Its Characteristics
excerpt_en: Now, I'll be answering questions that were submitted through the newsletter at the time! About religion in Malaysia: Malaysia is a multi-ethnic nation with major ethnic groups including Malay, Chinese, and Indian populations. Religions include Islam, Buddhism...
body_en:
Now, I'll be answering questions

that were submitted through the newsletter at the time!

About Religion in Malaysia

Malaysia is a multi-ethnic nation with major ethnic groups including Malay, Chinese, and Indian populations.

Religions include Islam, Buddhism, Christianity, Hinduism, Confucianism, and Taoism, with Islam as the national religion.

Each ethnic group has its own culture and customs,

which are also reflected in their religions.

Background of Islam Becoming the National Religion

The background lies in the Malacca Sultanate, established at the end of the 14th century, becoming an Islamic state.

After invasions by Portugal and Britain, Christianity was introduced, and further Chinese and Indian immigrants settled in the country.

Holidays and Religion

Malaysia is a multi-religious nation,

and festivals of each religion are treated as national holidays.

Birthdays of each religion's founders and new year celebrations are also treated as holidays.

Rules of Islam

  • There are rules regarding appearance, behavior, and food,

and these rules are respected in Malaysia.

For example,

in Islam the left hand is considered impure,

so the right hand is used more often.

This time, I didn't have the opportunity to work with any Muslims, but thinking they might find it strange, I consciously tried to use my right hand as much as possible.

Also, pointing at things with your index finger is apparently not good,

so when indicating a place or direction,

instead of using the index finger,

I used a sort of hand-waving gesture.

And the act of patting someone's head, which Japanese people often do --

this is considered rude outside of Japan, so you need to be careful.

*I didn't have anyone to do that to, so it wasn't an issue for me lol

Also, when visiting a mosque, women should not expose their skin.

At tourist mosques, they lend you a robe,

so wearing that is perfectly fine!

Summary of Malaysian Traditional Clothing

Malaysia's multiculturalism and traditional clothing

Malaysia is a multi-ethnic nation,

and walking around the streets, you can see a variety of traditional clothing.

The clothing of women from the three main ethnic groups -- Malay, Chinese, and Indian -- each differs.

Malay Clothing

The main garment for women is the "Baju Kurung."

This is an everyday garment designed not to expose the chest, arms, or legs, in accordance with Islamic law.

The traditional garment for men is the "Baju Melayu," often worn at special occasions and Friday prayers.

Indigenous People's Clothing

Malaysia's indigenous people are called "Orang Asli," and their clothing is distinctive and colorful.

These garments

reflect Malaysia's traditional way of life and culture.

Chinese Clothing

The traditional garment for women is the "Cheongsam." It's particularly common to wear this on special occasions like Chinese New Year.

Indian Clothing

The main garment for women is the "Sari."

This involves wrapping a cloth over 5 meters long around the body, and is a traditional Indian garment.

Malaysia's "National Dress"

Malaysia's representative garment is "Batik."

These are shirts and dresses made with wax-resist dyeing, often worn at official events and on special days.

The Charm and Character of Malaysian Cuisine

Malaysia is a multi-ethnic nation,

and that diversity is reflected in its cuisine.

Malaysian cuisine is influenced by the diverse cultures of Malay, Chinese, Indian, Thai, Javanese, and Sumatran traditions, and the fusion of these different cultures has given birth to dishes with unique flavors and character.

- Nasi Lemak

A representative Malaysian dish where freshly steamed white rice is wrapped with chicken curry. It's served with various accompaniments, and different regional variations can be enjoyed.

- Roti Canai

A traditional dish from Indian-Malaysians. Thinly stretched bread is grilled and enjoyed with curry sauce or other sauces.

- Laksa

A traditional Malaysian noodle dish

combining spicy soup with noodles.

Penang Assam Laksa features a sour soup based on fish bouillon and tamarind.

Toppings include shrimp, squid, chicken, bean sprouts, and herbs.

Malaysian Chinese Cuisine

- Malaysia is home to many Chinese people,

and their influence means you can enjoy diverse Chinese cuisine.

- In addition to traditional Chinese dishes,

there are also dishes with unique Malaysian twists.

Popular dishes include char siu (roast pork), wonton noodles, dim sum, and steamed fish.

Chinese cuisine can be enjoyed throughout Malaysia in Chinatowns like Petaling Street in Kuala Lumpur and Georgetown in Penang.

Malaysian cuisine is born from the fusion of Malay, Chinese, Indian, Islamic, and indigenous cultures, and its diversity and uniqueness are its greatest charm.

Each region and ethnic group has its own distinctive dishes,

and every visit lets you discover new flavors.

Personally, since laksa is something I rarely get to eat in Japan, I actively sought it out,

but every time it was so spicy that I ended up drenched in sweat lol

What gave me peace of mind was

the fried rice at food courts in shopping malls --

when I wanted to eat quickly, I'd dash right in!

Prices were around 300-600 yen,

so it's very wallet-friendly!

Summary

What did you think?

This time I covered religion, clothing, and food.

In terms of religion, there wasn't anything that particularly troubled me.

I just made a conscious effort not to use my index finger when changing shooting locations, I suppose?

I also heard that men shouldn't initiate handshakes with women, so I was careful about that too.

#On the first day, a masu model initiated a handshake with me

#Apparently it's okay from woman to man

As for clothing,

being such a multi-ethnic nation,

people in all kinds of traditional clothing were walking around,

and personally it was exciting.

Because they respect each other's religious views,

they're able to coexist.

As for food,

I didn't get an upset stomach at all.

Oh, as for toilets though,

Japan wins hands down.

Whether 4-star or 5-star,

there were no toilets with bidet seats.

Coming back to Japan, I'm feeling the gratitude lol

Malaysia is

consistently ranked high on the list of countries Japanese people want to live in.

In urban areas, Japanese food and Japanese snacks are readily available, and many Japanese companies have expanded there, so daily life shouldn't be a problem.

Plus, the time difference is only 1 hour,

so meetings with people in Japan were hardly an issue!

As for alcohol, Muslims don't drink,

but tourists drink like crazy.

Night markets are beer everywhere you look!

It was only 10 days,

but I came to love Malaysia.

I hope you all visit too!

And at Pavilion shopping mall's "Matcha Hero" and the sushi restaurant "Sushi Oribe,"

you'll find FOMUS masu,

so please stop by!

title_en: When in Rome, Do as the Romans Do
excerpt_en: In the morning, I headed to language school. Classes start at 9, so considering the train schedule, I need to leave by 8:10, but breakfast at my homestay is set at 8:00. I negotiated to change it to 7:50...
body_en:
In the morning, I headed to language school.

Classes start at 9,

so considering the train schedule,

I need to leave by 8:10,

but breakfast at my homestay

is set at "8:00,"

so I negotiated to change it to 7:50.

That said, I was hoping I could start eating at 7:45, so I went to the living room at 7:40, ten minutes early,

and was told "it's not ready yet."

Since I want to value the relationship

with my homestay family,

I went a little early, but

they have 4 dogs,

so they need time to

take the dogs from the living room

out to the yard,

and they want me to come right on time.

I don't know yet if this is

a house rule

or an Irish custom,

but in a way, having things set at exact times is nice!

Before coming to Ireland, in Spain,

I stayed at a nudist house and was told

"wearing clothes makes us uncomfortable,"

so when living with someone,

it's important to understand the rules properly.

This week will probably be about

figuring out what's okay and what's not.

In other words,

"when in Rome, do as the Romans do."

If I don't know the rules,

I might cause trouble without realizing it, so for now, I just have to live here and figure things out.

That was my lesson from the morning.

Drawbacks of the Train

From the nearest station to my homestay

to the station nearest the school is 6 stops, about 20 minutes.

Start eating breakfast at 7:50,

leave after 8.

If I catch the train by 8:20,

I won't be late.

However, the train hit me with a rude awakening right away.

Yes, a delay.

A full 20 minutes late!!

By the time I arrived at the school's nearest station, it was 8:56.

And from there, it's an 18-minute walk to the school.

Not wanting to be too late,

I sprinted from the station.

Thanks to my training for the Medoc Marathon,

I'm in better running shape than ever,

and who knew it would come in handy here lol

In the end, I was only 10 minutes late.

Normally, to avoid this risk,

you'd just move breakfast earlier,

but since this is a homestay,

that's not easy to change.

I already pushed to move the 8 AM breakfast by just 10 minutes,

and I'd rather not try to push it any earlier.

So, "if I'm going to be late, I run" -- that's now the plan lol

Change the Teacher

Classes run from 9 AM to 1 PM.

Teachers switch every 2 hours.

The first teacher is easy to understand and fun, but the second teacher was a miss.

I could tell this class wouldn't improve my English.

Here are some reasons:

1. Everything is slow

2. Gives students worksheets and then plays on their phone

3. After solving problems and doing group answer checks, types on the computer but

fusses over colors and font sizes, and the wasted time really bothers me

4. To explain the word "Car,"

draws a ridiculously detailed picture.

If it were to teach the names of car parts, I'd understand, but the teacher obsesses over strange details,

and then erases the drawing as soon as the question is done,

so for someone like me who thinks "time is more valuable than anything," it's really frustrating lol

So,

I used my signature move: changing teachers.

I was changing teachers every week in the Philippines too,

so I never thought I'd be doing it in Ireland as well.

And also on the 2nd day, no less lol

At the Ireland school,

apparently the class change takes effect starting tomorrow.

Nice system!

*In the Philippines, it was starting the following week

The School Cafe

I heard there's a cafe attached to the school,

so I went to check it out.

Ireland has high prices,

and even lunch costs a pretty penny.

Plus it's a tourist area, so it's even pricier.

So I desperately want to cut lunch costs.

That's why I headed to the school cafe.

They had food for 2 to 5 euros.

I want to try everything eventually,

so today I went with the cheapest option: a 2-euro hot dog.

It was a totally ordinary hot dog.

But with this, I successfully slashed my lunch costs.

Joining an Event

This school holds events

3-4 times a week,

and I participated in one this time too.

About 15 of us

rode a bus.

About a 20-minute bus ride to a botanical garden.

Honestly, I'm not interested in botanical gardens.

But my goal is to connect with other language school students.

The benefit of events is

that you can meet people

outside of your regular class.

For someone like me,

that's an incredibly valuable perk!

A mix of Europeans and Asians gathered.

By the way, the youngest was 17.

The oldest was me lol

Still, with this look,

standing side by side,

I easily pass as a student too lol

Maybe my daily skincare routine is paying off lol

Train Drawback #2

When everything was done

and I tried to head home

via the same route as yesterday,

I wasn't paying attention to the destination,

and ended up going west instead of north.

I was supposed to take the light green route,

but ended up on the dark green one.

When I later asked my host mother,

she told me there are two different train lines,

and because I hadn't checked the electronic display board,

I made the mistake.

Next time, I'll make sure to check the destination

before boarding the train.

I ended up at a completely unknown station

excerpt_en: It's the last day of July! Time really flies. Today, I'll be writing about "going to a Japan-Ireland exchange event." It's my 3rd day at language school in Ireland. But I already changed teachers yesterday, so today I start with a new class.
body_en:
It's the last day of July!

Time really flies.

Today,

I'll be writing about "going to a Japan-Ireland exchange event."

It's my 3rd day at language school in Ireland.

But I already changed teachers yesterday,

so today I start with a new class.

The teacher isn't bad per se,

but it's a textbook-based class

that mainly focuses on grammar.

Grammar is important, of course,

but I didn't come to Ireland

to do textbook work.

I already covered the basics

during my language study in the Philippines,

so I felt like I could skip this level.

Also, the English level of the people around me

is definitely lower than mine.

I'd rather be in an environment where I think,

"Oh no! I can't keep up!

I need to listen way more carefully!!"

That drives me.

So,

after class I went to request

a class change again,

but while changing teachers is possible,

leveling up the class

requires that teacher's permission.

So,

I need to attend the same class tomorrow

and get permission.

Everyone has already bought the textbook,

but I'm the only one who hasn't lol

I'm being very careful about this.

And out of the 12 weeks of classes,

I'll use the first week to get a feel for things, then customize my surroundings to make the most of the remaining 11 weeks.

I'm not the kind of person to go along with the crowd.

To a Japan-Ireland Exchange Event

GPS runner Shimizu-san,

knowing I was going to Ireland,

introduced me to Nick, an Irish person living in Japan.

Nick messaged me in the morning saying,

"There's an event like this, you should check it out."

It was

a Japanese-Irish exchange event!

For someone like me who has

zero connections in Ireland,

I absolutely need to get into

local communities.

So,

I jumped in on short notice!!

It really was mostly Japanese and Irish people!!

Honestly, it had been a while since

I'd spoken so much Japanese at an in-person event.

Of course, about half were Irish,

so I chatted with them too.

I learned that sure enough, Temple Bar (the famous pub district)

has no locals -- just tourists.

For real information about Ireland,

the best source is people

who are actually living there.

For example, the topic of renting a place.

In 2 months,

I'll be leaving my homestay

and living on my own,

so I'll need a place.

I'd heard that house-hunting is extremely difficult,

and I was thinking I'd need to start early,

but hearing the real stories confirmed

that I really should start preparing sooner.

Starting at 7 PM, we mingled until about 10:30 PM,

drinking Asahi beer at the venue!

After that, I went out for dinner with Japanese people I'd met there.

It was a meal with people who've been here for about 3 years already,

so I learned a ton.

From the perspective of someone who's been in Ireland for just 4 days,

everyone is a senpai.

And apparently, someone on their 4th day showing up to this event is pretty rare.

*You wouldn't normally even find out about it

I was fortunate enough

to be told about this event,

which is how I could participate.

Thanks to that, in less than a week,

I made both Japanese and Irish friends.

Having opportunities to meet people

outside of language school gatherings

is such a blessing!

I'll continue to regularly attend events like this!

excerpt_en: Well, August is here! Only 4 months left in the year! Time to buckle down. I want to change my class level, but... It's now the 4th day of classes. I've already changed classes once, but honestly, this class doesn't suit me either.
body_en:
Well, August is here!

Only 4 months left in the year! Time to buckle down.

I want to change my class level, but...

It's now the 4th day of classes.

I've already changed classes once,

but honestly,

this class doesn't suit me either.

It's textbook-based English study

with very little speaking time,

so it's not the kind of class I'm looking for.

On top of that, the content is pretty basic,

and I'm like, "Why are we still doing this?!"

about the things being covered.

I wanted to move up one class level,

so I went to the office,

and yesterday they told me to get permission from the teacher. So today I asked,

and was told, "You just came to this class yesterday, so I can't tell yet. Give it about 2 more weeks and I'll evaluate."

Honestly, I'm only at this school for 12 weeks,

so I can't afford to wait that long lol

Tomorrow I'll attend the same class one more time,

then try to get a different teacher.

If I get an understanding teacher,

I should be able to move up quickly.

Meetings with Japan

Right after class ended,

I had a meeting about FOMUS activities in Japan.

The time difference between Japan and Ireland is 7 hours.

When it's morning in Japan, it's the middle of the night in Ireland.

When I'm overseas

and need to have meetings with people in Japan,

I basically ask to do them in the evening.

The reason is that I don't have an environment where I can talk loudly at night.

I'm in a homestay right now,

and I basically need to be quiet after 10 PM.

So from about 7 AM to 1 PM Japan time,

I can't do voice meetings.

Plus, I'm also attending classes in Ireland, which takes up another 4 hours.

So,

meetings inevitably end up being after 10 PM Japan time.

Since the people I work with aren't really the 9-to-5 type, nobody has a problem with meetings at night, which is a lifesaver lol

Anyway,

it was a meeting about the creator division for the container gallery that FOMUS is building on Sagi Island.

The operations team and

the creator team are separate.

Because their "roles" are different.

So the meeting members are different too.

I participate in both.

This time, we discussed the artwork for the container's ceiling.

A lot of progress was made!

Once again, I'm so grateful to my teammates who keep things moving domestically.

That's exactly why

I have the responsibility to deliver results overseas.

This pressure

is what drives me,

so there's no room to slack off.

In the creator team meetings too,

since I've been going around

seeing top-tier artwork these past few years,

my perspective has shifted,

leading to more suggestions based on that.

Seeing the real thing and first-class content is incredibly important.

Since this meeting started right after school, I pushed through without even eating lunch.

After getting home, it was non-stop work.

Every day, I'm somehow busy with activities.

With a nomadic lifestyle,

desk work actually gets done better overseas.

Working with Japan has drawbacks like

the time difference and not being able to meet face-to-face,

but otherwise, work efficiency is surprisingly

better with this kind of setup because I can focus!

It's not about which is better --

it depends on the situation,

but so far, I'm even enjoying that aspect.

I'll keep at it.

https://www.notion.so

excerpt_en: 08/02. In the morning I attended language school, and in the afternoon I came home early to work on FOMUS. Since the shoots in America and Spain are already done, I want to get the Masu Photo photobook made as soon as possible.
body_en:
08/02

In the morning I attended language school,

and in the afternoon I came home early to work on FOMUS.

Since the shoots in America and Spain are already done, I want to get the Masu Photo photobook made as soon as possible.

Or rather,

this photobook is also a reward for our crowdfunding backers, so I want to work on it whenever I have even a little time.

Layout, composition, photo selection --

I'm basically doing all the work by myself.

This is something I can't delegate to anyone else,

since I'm the one who shot and edited everything.

As for the cover layout,

the first photobook in 2022 was designed by professional designer Kankan-san,

but from the second one, the Taiwan edition onward,

I've been creating all the covers myself.

I can't do specialized work like logo design, but collages are something

I can handle since I've made plenty of pamphlets and posters in the past.

So,

I'll be making the cover for this America edition too.

This time the stay was shorter,

so there were only about 10 models.

It was tough getting people together.

I learned firsthand that recruiting models in America

is quite a high hurdle.

That said, making a photobook is definitely possible,

so I'll keep chipping away at it!

excerpt_en: 08/03. The language school had a tour to the Cliffs of Moher, a scenic spot about 4 hours by bus from Dublin, so I joined. The cost was 65 euros, about 11,000 yen. Apparently it's a bit cheaper than regular tours.
body_en:
08/03

The language school had a tour to the Cliffs of Moher,

a scenic spot about 4 hours by bus from Dublin,

so I joined.

The cost was 65 euros.

About 11,000 yen.

Apparently it's a bit cheaper than regular tours.

Plus,

you ride the same bus as other students from the school.

There were people from Brazil, Germany, Spain, and more --

it was really fun.

We arrived at 1:30 PM.

The bus return was at 3 PM,

so 4 hours of travel for just 1.5 hours of sightseeing lol

Well, I guess that's how it goes lol

At first, I went around with the other students,

but my goal wasn't sightseeing.

It was socializing and shooting Masu-Game photos.

I probably won't come here again.

For me, seeing the scenery once is enough.

"Masu-Game x world landmarks" photos

have proven useful for various future projects, so I basically take photos at every tourist spot.

This time too, that's how I spent my time.

At one point, an absolutely insane amount of rain came down,

forcing me to stop shooting and put Masu-Game back in my backpack, but I managed to get the shots done.

Also, this place is really vast,

and you need to walk quite a bit to see everything,

so I skipped lunch and just explored.

I could have chosen to eat with everyone,

but for me, shooting with Masu-Game takes higher priority than eating,

so I skipped lunch lol

But thanks to that, I got some great photos.

Then, another 4 hours back to Dublin city,

and from there I took the train home.

I was absolutely starving,

but I ate at home, so it was essentially free.

The food in Ireland,

to be honest, isn't great,

so "hunger is the best spice."

And so,

it was a full day spent on a tour making friends and shooting Masu-Game -- a very productive day.

Personally, I wish I'd socialized a bit more,

but there weren't many people I could talk business with,

so there were moments I just wasn't interested enough.

That's a bad habit of mine --

I need to take more interest in other people.

I'll reflect on that.

I still have a long time in Ireland,

so I'll work on improving this!

excerpt_en: 08/04. At the Japan-Ireland event the other day, I heard there's a meetup event on Sundays, so I went to check it out, but there were no Japanese people and it didn't seem like the event was happening, so I changed plans.
body_en:
08/04

At the Japan-Ireland event the other day,

I heard that a meetup event is held on Sundays,

so I went to check it out,

but there were no Japanese people there, and it didn't seem like any event was happening, so I changed plans.

It was raining,

so I thought about going to a nearby Irish pub, but sure enough, it was Sunday.

Every place along Temple Bar was packed.

No room to get in.

I realized that going for drinks on a weekday during early hours is a must.

So,

another change of plans.

Off to the National Gallery of Ireland.

In America and Spain, art was all paid, but for some reason in Ireland it's free.

I even got to see a Vermeer piece.

Being able to see stuff like this for free is wonderful.

I spent about 2 hours looking around,

then headed home.

It's been one week

since I arrived in Dublin, Ireland.

I've tried to go to as many social gatherings as possible,

but I haven't yet made any connections that could lead to business.

That said, nothing happens

if you don't meet people,

so this week I'll continue actively participating in events.
